---
title: "synthesim: an interactive fitness-fatigue model explorer"
description: "A deterministic forward-simulator for the MEDv4 fitness-fatigue ODE with a seven-test physiological-plausibility validator. R Shiny, marimo WASM, Docker, MIT-licensed."
pagetitle: "synthesim case study | Jacob Bowie, PhD"
date: 2026-05-12
categories: [system dynamics, ODE, R, Python, Shiny, marimo, fitness-fatigue]
format:
  html:
    page-layout: article
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: right
    code-fold: false
    smooth-scroll: true
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Try it:** [Live Shiny demo](https://get-paid.shinyapps.io/synthesim/) ·
[Repository](https://github.com/JacobBowie/synthesim) ·
MIT licensed · `v0.1.0` released 2026-05-12.
:::

![Reference-mode test RM1: doubled training dose produces roughly twice the rate of fitness gain over 52 weeks. The dose-response gate the simulator must satisfy.](../img/synthesim-rm1-dose-response.png){fig-alt="Two line plots showing fitness gain trajectories for a baseline training dose and a doubled dose over 52 weeks, illustrating that the doubled-dose curve rises at roughly twice the rate."}

## The problem

The Banister fitness-fatigue model and its descendants have been the
methodological scaffolding for fifty years of resistance-training adaptation
research. The math is simple enough to fit in a slide and old enough to
predate modern hardware. What is missing is a working artifact that researchers,
coaches, and reviewers can pick up and *play with* to develop intuition: how
does a doubled training dose change the long-run fitness trajectory? At what
point does additional load produce diminishing returns? How quickly does
detraining erase a year of accumulated work?

The literature has plenty of papers fitting parameters to data. It has very
few sandboxes for asking what-if questions about the dynamics.

`synthesim` is the sandbox.

## What it is

A deterministic forward-simulator for the MEDv4 fitness-fatigue ODE, a
three-stock system-dynamics model that ports cleanly from the Vensim implementations
in the established literature into R and Python. The same model logic ships
through three runtimes from one Docker image:

- An **R Shiny application** for click-and-explore use
- A **marimo notebook** that runs entirely in the browser via Pyodide (no
  local Python required)
- A **headless validator script** that runs the seven reference-mode tests on
  every change to the model

The headline interaction is the dose-response slider: change the weekly
training load, watch fitness, fatigue, and net performance respond in real
time over a 52-week simulation horizon. The interaction is fast enough to
*feel* the dynamic structure of the model, not just read about it.

## The seven-test plausibility validator

The methodological heart of the project is not the simulator. It is the
validator that runs every time the simulator changes.

A fitness-fatigue model has to satisfy a set of qualitative physiological
constraints before it deserves to be fit to data. These are not noise-bound
empirical claims; they are structural commitments. The seven tests in
`synthesim` encode them:

1. **RM1: dose response.** Doubling training dose roughly doubles the
   long-run rate of fitness gain.
2. **RM2: detraining.** Removing all training produces a slow exponential
   decay back to the activities-of-daily-living floor.
3. **RM3: saturation.** Holding load constant produces a long-run plateau,
   not unbounded growth.
4. **RM4: fatigue dominance.** A sudden spike in load produces a transient
   net-performance dip before any fitness gain materializes.
5. **RM5: ADL floor.** Fitness does not fall below the activities-of-daily-living
   baseline regardless of how long detraining runs.
6. **RM6: taper.** Reducing load just before a target date produces a
   net-performance peak above the loaded-state value.
7. **RM7: overreach.** Sustained high load past a structural threshold
   pushes fatigue past fitness; net performance goes negative.

Each test is a one-page reference mode with a target trajectory the simulator
must reproduce to within a configurable tolerance. The validator is the
structural gate before anything more ambitious (Bayesian fitting against a
real cohort, hierarchical extensions, individualized priors) gets built on
top of the model.

## How it is built

The single source of truth is a small R package wrapping the ODE solve and
the seven validators. The Shiny app, the marimo notebook, and the headless
test runner all import from that package, so a change to the model logic
propagates to all three surfaces at once. The Docker image pins R, Python,
marimo, and Shiny Server in one reproducible environment; a single
`docker compose up` brings the explorer online locally.

The repository ships with a `CITATION.cff` file so researchers can cite the
specific version they explored, a pinned dependency manifest for both R and
Python, and a `Dockerfile` whose layer caching keeps the rebuild loop short.

## Where this fits

`synthesim` is the public face of a larger, currently private Bayesian
fitness-fatigue project (working name `getpaid`) that aims to fit the same
MEDv4 model hierarchically against an open resistance-training cohort. The
public artifact and the private codebase share the deterministic core; the
private codebase adds Stan, priors derived from a 175-paper citation review,
and per-subject partial pooling.

The paired companion is **The Banister Constellation**, a 175-paper citation
graph of fifty years of fitness-fatigue model literature that anchors the
priors and frames the lineage. The Constellation is previewed on the
[portfolio homepage](../index.html); interactive viewer and source release in
progress.

## Try it

The fastest path is the live demo:

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**[Live Shiny demo at get-paid.shinyapps.io/synthesim](https://get-paid.shinyapps.io/synthesim/)**

Open it in a new tab, run the seven validator tests (top tab), then return
to the dose-response slider and try to break the model. The validator is
the part to scrutinize.
:::

For local use, `docker compose up` from the cloned repo brings the same
explorer online in about thirty seconds.

## Source and citation

- Repository: [github.com/JacobBowie/synthesim](https://github.com/JacobBowie/synthesim)
- License: MIT
- Citation: see `CITATION.cff` in the repository for the canonical form

If `synthesim` is useful in your own work, please reach out at
**[jacob.bowie2@gmail.com](mailto:jacob.bowie2@gmail.com)**. I am especially
interested in hearing from coaches and applied scientists who have qualitative
reference modes the validator does not yet capture.
